questions.q: What is the order of the memory hierarchy from fastest to slowest in a typical system?
What is the common x86 page size?

questions.a: A shared page is duplicated only when one process writes to it.

questions.q: What does mmap() let a process do?
questions.a: Map files or anonymous memory into its virtual address space.
The fixed-size block moved between memory and cache.
They interrupt execution so the kernel can map in the missing page from RAM or disk.

questions.q: Why does locality matter for performance?
questions.a: It lets the CPU reuse data already fetched into cache or nearby memory.

questions.q: Why does a TLB miss slow down address translation?
questions.a: The CPU must walk page tables instead of using a cached virtual-to-physical translation.

questions.q: What problem does virtual memory solve?
questions.a: It gives each process an isolated, large, contiguous address space independent of physical RAM layout.

questions.q: Why can sequential access be much faster than random access?
questions.a: Prefetching and cache lines work well when accesses are predictable and contiguous.
Keeping memory access close to the CPU socket that uses it most.

questions.q: When do huge pages help, and when do they hurt?
questions.a: They help when TLB pressure is high, but they can waste memory and reduce flexibility.

questions.q: Why can a memory allocator influence application latency?
questions.a: Allocator behavior affects fragmentation, locality, lock contention, and page fault frequency.

questions.q: How do you choose between more RAM and a better access pattern?
questions.a: Fix the access pattern first if misses are avoidable; buy RAM when the working set is truly too large.

questions.q: Why can mmap be preferable to read for large files?
questions.a: It lets the kernel page data in on demand and can reduce copies for selective access.

questions.q: What tradeoff do cache-friendly data layouts introduce?
questions.a: They often improve speed but can make code harder to maintain or extend.

questions.q: Why does CoW help fork-heavy programs?
questions.a: It avoids copying pages until they are modified, saving time and memory for read-mostly workloads.
